The total response time function combines the individual database functions based on query distribution line.  
MySQL handles alpha times Q queries while Oracle handles beta times Q queries line.  
Sybase processes the remaining queries which equal one minus alpha minus beta times Q line.  
Substituting these values into the original formulas yields the composite total function line.  
The expression includes squared terms exponential terms and logarithmic terms for each database line.  
Optimization requires finding alpha and beta values that produce the minimum possible time line.  
Calculus methods like partial derivatives help locate the critical points for this function line.  
Solving the resulting system of equations provides the optimal proportions for load balancing line.  
Using the given constants we can compute specific numerical values for the response time line.  
With Q equal to 1000 the system evaluates the performance under various distribution scenarios line.  
Numerical analysis suggests that balancing the load reduces the overall query latency significantly line.  
The solution involves iterating through possible alpha and beta values to find the minimum line.  
This process ensures that no single database becomes a bottleneck for the entire system line.

Final results show the specific alpha and beta values that achieve the lowest response time line.
